text: The surgeon places the standard camera port and three additional ports for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with a right tilt. The fundus of the gallbladder is grasped and pushed toward the right shoulder, clearing fibrofatty connective tissue. The neck of the gallbladder is then grasped and pushed down toward the right hip, opening the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon demonstrates the safety line, running from the Rouvier's sulcus to the umbilical fissure at the bottom of segment four of the liver. The Rouvier's sulcus is visible on the undersurface of the right lobe of the liver. Dissection above this line is considered safe. The critical view of safety is achieved by clearing the hepatocystic triangle of fat and fibrous tissue, separating the lowest part of the gallbladder from the cystic plate, and ensuring only the cystic artery and duct are seen entering the gallbladder.

The surgeon clips and divides the cystic artery and cystic duct after achieving the critical view of safety. The neck of the gallbladder is initially pulled upward, causing misalignment with the common bile duct, but is then adjusted to display the critical view adequately. The gallbladder is subsequently removed.

Fibrofatty tissue is visible creeping onto the body of the gallbladder. The surgeon pulls the neck of the gallbladder upward to dissect in the posterior part of the hepatocystic triangle, ensuring the safety line is visible while avoiding blind dissection.

The surgeon encounters small bleeding branches from the cystic artery, creating a messy surgical field. The gallbladder wall appears fibrous and thick, making it challenging to grasp the fundus. The surgeon uses suction to remove the gallbladder's contents, facilitating fundus grasping. Fibro fatty tissue is removed by pulling the gallbladder upwards, ensuring the safety line is visible and dissection is performed cephalid. The cystic duct is partially covered with fibro fatty tissue, and the recommendation is to skeletonize it before clipping and division.
The surgeon locates the cystic artery posterior to the fibrofatty tissue within the hepatocystic triangle, using clips to secure it safely despite the incomplete clearance of fibrofatty structures.

The surgeon carefully identifies the short cystic duct, which is challenging due to the pendulous and bulky nature of the gallbladder. The dissection is performed above the safety line within the hepatocystic triangle to avoid harm to vital structures.
